truth.papers.A4.title: Enhanced mechanical properties of a Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel by increasing hot-rolling reduction
truth.papers.A4.paper_scope: The microstructure evolution and mechanical properties of a Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel at different hot-rolling reductions are investigated, and the strengthening and strain-hardening mechanisms are analyzed.

prediction.papers.A4.title: Enhanced mechanical properties of a Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel by increasing hot-rolling reduction
prediction.papers.A4.paper_scope: Investigation of microstructure evolution, mechanical properties, strengthening mechanisms, and strain-hardening behavior of a Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel at hot-rolling reductions of 45%, 68%, and 82%.

truth.alloys.alloy_fe31mn9al1c_nb45mo45w45.all: Actual composition (wt%): Mn 30.9, Al 8.7, C 0.9, Nb 0.45, Mo 0.45, W 0.43, Fe balance. Density measured: 6.82 g/cm³, ~13% density reduction compared to pure iron.

prediction.alloys.alloy_fe31mn9al1c_nb45mo45w45.all: Nominal composition is Fe-31Mn-9Al-1C-0.45Nb-0.45Mo-0.45W (wt%). Measured density is 6.82 g/cm³, corresponding to a density reduction of ~13% compared to pure iron.

truth.processes.proc_fe31mn9_45.description: Hot-forged billet reheated at 1150°C, hot-rolled to a reduction of 45% (thickness 60 mm) in 4 passes, followed by water quenching.
truth.processes.proc_fe31mn9_45.processes_note: Forged from ingot at 1150°C to 110 mm billet and air cooled prior to reheating.
truth.processes.proc_fe31mn9_68.description: Hot-forged billet reheated at 1150°C, hot-rolled to a reduction of 68% (thickness 35 mm) in 6 passes, followed by water quenching.
truth.processes.proc_fe31mn9_82.description: Hot-forged billet reheated at 1150°C, hot-rolled to a reduction of 82% (thickness 20 mm) in 9 passes, followed by water quenching.

prediction.processes.proc_fe31mn9_45.description: Vacuum induction melting, hot-forged at 1150°C into 110 mm billets, air cooled; reheated to 1150°C, hot-rolled with 45% reduction to 60 mm plate over 4 passes, water quenched to room temperature.
prediction.processes.proc_fe31mn9_68.description: Vacuum induction melting, hot-forged at 1150°C into 110 mm billets, air cooled; reheated to 1150°C, hot-rolled with 68% reduction to 35 mm plate over 6 passes, water quenched to room temperature.
prediction.processes.proc_fe31mn9_82.description: Vacuum induction melting, hot-forged at 1150°C into 110 mm billets, air cooled; reheated to 1150°C, hot-rolled with 82% reduction to 20 mm plate over 9 passes, water quenched to room temperature.

prediction.processing_steps.sample_hr45.processing_: Rolled to 60 mm plate in 4 passes followed by water quenching to room temperature.
prediction.processing_steps.sample_hr68.processing_: Rolled to 35 mm plate in 6 passes followed by water quenching to room temperature.
prediction.processing_steps.sample_hr82.processing_: Rolled to 20 mm plate in 9 passes followed by water quenching to room temperature.

truth.structures.sample_hr45.overall_structure: Equiaxed fully austenitic microstructure with annealing twins, containing nano-sized κ-carbides and (Nb,Mo,W)C particles.
truth.structures.sample_hr45.microstructure_li: Mean grain size excluding twins: 45.11 μm; effective grain size including twin boundaries: 21.82 μm; Σ3 twin boundary fraction: 67%
Submicron particles homogeneously distributed; micron-sized particles along rolling direction at grain boundaries and interior
truth.structures.sample_hr68.overall_structure: Equiaxed fully austenitic microstructure with annealing twins, containing nano-sized κ-carbides and (Nb,Mo,W)C particles.
truth.structures.sample_hr68.microstructure_li: Mean grain size excluding twins: 27.02 μm; effective grain size including twin boundaries: 11.55 μm; Σ3 twin boundary fraction: 60%
Submicron particles homogeneously distributed; micron-sized particles along rolling direction at grain boundaries and interior
truth.structures.sample_hr82.overall_structure: Equiaxed fully austenitic microstructure with annealing twins, containing nano-sized κ-carbides and (Nb,Mo,W)C particles.
truth.structures.sample_hr82.microstructure_li: Mean grain size excluding twins: 18.80 μm; effective grain size including twin boundaries: 7.02 μm; Σ3 twin boundary fraction: 52%
Submicron particles homogeneously distributed; micron-sized particles along rolling direction at grain boundaries and interior

prediction.structures.sample_hr45.overall_structure: Fully austenitic matrix (FCC) with equiaxed grains, a high fraction of first-order annealing twins, nano-sized κ-carbides, and micron/submicron (Nb,Mo,W)C particles.
prediction.structures.sample_hr45.microstructure_li: Equiaxed grains with a large fraction of first-order annealing twins
Nano-sized particles homogeneously precipitated inside austenite grains during water quenching
Micron-sized polygonal along rolling direction at grain boundaries/interiors and submicron-sized spherical in matrix
Along grain boundaries and in grain interior

truth.interfaces.sample_hr45.defect_interactio: Dislocations shear coherent κ-carbides, creating anti-phase boundaries and causing glide plane softening.
Dislocations bypass incoherent (Nb,Mo,W)C particles via Orowan mechanism.
truth.interfaces.sample_hr45.phase_evolution: With increasing hot-rolling reduction, grain size refined due to enhanced DRX and PSN, dislocation density increased, while precipitate types and volume fractions remained similar.
truth.interfaces.sample_hr68.defect_interactio: Dislocations shear coherent κ-carbides, creating anti-phase boundaries and causing glide plane softening.
Dislocations bypass incoherent (Nb,Mo,W)C particles via Orowan mechanism.
truth.interfaces.sample_hr82.defect_interactio: Dislocations shear coherent κ-carbides, creating anti-phase boundaries and causing glide plane softening.
Dislocations bypass incoherent (Nb,Mo,W)C particles via Orowan mechanism.

prediction.interfaces.sample_hr45.defect_interactio: Grain boundaries and twin boundaries as barriers to dislocation movement
High-angle grain boundaries and Σ3 (60°〈111〉) annealing twin boundaries act as effective impedance sites to dislocation motion and reduce mean free path.
prediction.interfaces.sample_hr45.interface_notes[0: Strain/stress concentration occurs at interfaces of intergranular (Nb,Mo,W)C particles due to deformation discrepancy with the austenite matrix; partially alleviated by debonding of intragranular particles and microvoid formation.
prediction.interfaces.sample_hr45.phase_evolution: κ-carbides maintained coherency with the austenite matrix in the as-rolled and water-quenched condition without aging-induced coarsening or intergranular network formation.
prediction.interfaces.sample_hr68.defect_interactio: Grain boundaries and twin boundaries as barriers to dislocation movement
High-angle grain boundaries and Σ3 (60°〈111〉) annealing twin boundaries act as effective impedance sites to dislocation motion and reduce mean free path.

prediction.interfaces.sample_hr68.phase_evolution: κ-carbides maintained coherency with the austenite matrix in the as-rolled and water-quenched condition without aging-induced coarsening or intergranular network formation.

High-angle grain boundaries and Σ3 (60°〈111〉) annealing twin boundaries act as effective impedance sites to dislocation motion and reduce mean free path.

prediction.interfaces.sample_hr82.phase_evolution: κ-carbides maintained coherency with the austenite matrix in the as-rolled and water-quenched condition without aging-induced coarsening or intergranular network formation.

truth.properties.sample_hr45.mechanical.tensil: Engineering stress-strain curves shown in Fig. 5a; true stress-strain and strain-hardening rate curves in Fig. 10.

prediction.properties.sample_hr45.mechanical.tensil: Engineering stress-strain curve shown in Fig. 5a; true stress-strain and strain-hardening rate curves shown in Fig. 10.
prediction.properties.sample_hr68.mechanical.tensil: Engineering stress-strain curve shown in Fig. 5a; true stress-strain and strain-hardening rate curves shown in Fig. 10.
prediction.properties.sample_hr82.mechanical.tensil: Engineering stress-strain curve shown in Fig. 5a; true stress-strain and strain-hardening rate curves shown in Fig. 10.

truth.performance.sample_hr82.comparative_perf: Solution-treated austenitic stainless steels (SUS347J1TB, Nickel-free ASS, Super ASS) and Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys (Fe-28Mn-9Al-0.8C, Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.4-1.2C etc.)

YS of HR82 comparable to aging-hardened steels (~650 MPa) but with significantly higher ductility (50% vs. 21%) and toughness (105 J/m^2 vs. 35 J/m^2); markedly higher YS than solution-treated ASS with similar ductility and toughness.

prediction.performance.sample_hr82.comparative_perf: Aging-hardened austenitic low-density steels and solution-treated austenitic stainless steels

Comparable yield strength (~650 MPa) to aging-hardened Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys but considerably better ductility (50% vs. ~21%) and toughness (105 J vs. ~35 J); markedly higher yield strength than solution-treated austenitic stainless steels and Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys while maintaining similar ductility (~50%) and toughness (~100 J/m²).

prediction.characterization_methods.sample_hr45.key: Charpy impact energy 133 ± 2.5 J/m²; typical ductile fracture with dimples and microvoids.
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_hr68.key: Charpy impact energy 116 ± 2.8 J/m²; typical ductile fracture with dimples and microvoids.
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_hr82.key: Charpy impact energy 105 ± 1.2 J/m²; typical ductile fracture with dimples and microvoids.

truth.computational_details.idx_0.analytical_m: Lattice constant a, Avogadro constant N_A, free energy difference ΔG^{γ→ε}, γ/ε interface energy σ
Calculated SFE ~73 mJ/m², indicating high SFE.
truth.computational_details.idx_1.validation_a: Calculated yield strengths (Table 2) show good agreement with experimental values.
truth.computational_details.idx_1.analytical_m: Equation (5) summarizing contributions from friction, precipitation, dislocation, and grain boundary strengthening; sub-models Eq. (6) (κ shearing), Eq. (7) ((Nb,Mo,W)C Orowan), Eq. (8) (dislocation), Eq. (9) (Hall-Petch).
σ0=203.1 MPa, Hall-Petch coefficient k_HP=733.43 MPa·μm^(1/2), Taylor factor M=3.06, shear moduli 68 GPa (κ) and 170 GPa (MC), Burgers vector b=0.26 nm, APB energy 350 mJ/m², Poisson's ratio ν.
Grain boundary strengthening (from HAGBs and twin boundaries) was the dominant mechanism, followed by dislocation strengthening.

prediction.computational_details.idx_0.analytical_m: Calculation of stacking fault energy Γ
a (lattice constant of austenite), N_A (Avogadro constant), ΔG^(γ→ε) (free energy difference), σ (γ/ε interface energy)
Calculated stacking fault energy (SFE) of the present steel is ~73 mJ/m².
prediction.computational_details.idx_1.validation_a: Calculated yield strengths show good consistency with experimental results summarized in Table 2.
prediction.computational_details.idx_1.analytical_m: Effective grain size including annealing twin boundaries was used; contributions: HR45 157.0 MPa, HR68 215.8 MPa, HR82 276.8 MPa.
M = 3.06, α = 0.2, G = 68 GPa, b = 0.26 nm, ρ_ds from KAM-derived GND density
GND densities used: HR45 2.71×10^13, HR68 7.49×10^13, HR82 9.87×10^13 m^-2; contributions: HR45 56.3 MPa, HR68 93.7 MPa, HR82 107.5 MPa.
M = 3.06, G = 68 GPa, b = 0.26 nm, γ_APB ~350 mJ/m²
Shearing mechanism contributions: HR45 ~60.2 MPa, HR68 ~58.0 MPa, HR82 ~60.2 MPa; did not contribute to strength increment between reductions.
M = 3.06, G = 170 GPa, b = 0.26 nm, ν (Poisson's ratio)
Submicron-sized particle contributions: HR45 ~6.4 MPa, HR68 ~6.7 MPa, HR82 ~6.5 MPa; micron-sized contribution negligible.

truth.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: Slip band spacing refined with strain, saturating at ~50 nm for all samples; earlier saturation in finer-grained samples led to earlier microband formation and slight ductility loss.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_1.value: Three-staged strain-hardening behavior: initial drop (stage I) due to glide plane softening, increase (stage II) from slip band refinement and long-range stress, slow decline (stage III) from microband formation.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_2.value: Fractography: all samples showed ductile fracture with dimples and microvoids; intergranular (Nb,Mo,W)C particles act as microcrack nucleation sites, but effect mitigated by debonding of intragranular particles.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: KAM maps (Fig. 1g-i) indicate higher local strain concentrations with increasing rolling reduction, consistent with higher GND densities.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_4.value: Fig. 10 shows strain-hardening rate curves; the rate maintained ~2000 MPa over a wide strain range, contributing to excellent ductility.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_5.value: No image pixel data available for figures; conclusions based on text descriptions of micrographs and graphs.

prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: Uniform elongation (UE) from engineering stress-strain curves: HR45 52.0 ± 1.0%, HR68 47.0 ± 0.5%, HR82 39.0 ± 0.8%.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_1.value: Reduction of area from tensile tests: HR45 63.0 ± 1.0%, HR68 63.0 ± 0.0%, HR82 61.5 ± 0.3%.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_2.value: Charpy V-notch (CVN) impact energy at room temperature: HR45 133 ± 2.5 J/m², HR68 116 ± 2.8 J/m², HR82 105 ± 1.2 J/m².
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: Effective grain sizes including annealing twin boundaries (Σ3): HR45 ~21.82 μm, HR68 ~11.55 μm, HR82 ~7.02 μm.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_4.value: Annealing twin boundary length fractions relative to total HAGBs (>15°): HR45 67%, HR68 60%, HR82 52%.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_5.value: Evolution of slip band spacing with engineering strain (Fig. 12): at strain 0.1, HR45 296.40 ± 50.06 nm, HR68 155.02 ± 38.42 nm, HR82 68.05 ± 8.91 nm; saturation limit reached at ~50 nm at higher strains.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_6.value: Deformation substructures during interrupted tensile tests (Fig. 11) showed planar slip bands and Taylor lattice at low strain, transitioning to microbands with ill-defined and distinct boundaries at higher strains; finer-grained samples exhibited faster substructure evolution and earlier microband formation.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_7.value: Fractography (Fig. 6) of all hot-rolled samples showed typical ductile fracture with ductile dimples and microvoids; dimple size decreased with increasing rolling reduction due to grain refinement, and intergranular (Nb,Mo,W)C particles played no detrimental effect on toughness.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_8.value: Summary of calculated strengthening contributions to yield strength (Table 2): HR45 — σ_0 203.1, σ_p-κ 61.0, σ_p-(Nb,Mo,W)C 6.5, σ_ds 56.3, σ_gb 157.0 (calc 483.9, exp 480.0) MPa; HR68 — σ_0 203.1, σ_p-κ 58.0, σ_p-(Nb,Mo,W)C 6.7, σ_ds 93.7, σ_gb 215.8 (calc 577.3, exp 577.0) MPa; HR82 — σ_0 203.1, σ_p-κ 60.2, σ_p-(Nb,Mo,W)C 6.4, σ_ds 107.5, σ_gb 276.8 (calc 653.9, exp 648.0) MPa.
